result:
The Persistence of Small Joys

There's a particular shade of light that happens only in late September, when the sun has begun its retreat from summer's dominance but hasn't yet surrendered to autumn's austerity. It filters through leaves that haven't quite turned, gilding edges that will soon be bronze and crimson. I found myself standing in this light yesterday, waiting for nothing in particular, and felt an unexpected surge of what I can only describe as uncomplicated presence—the rare sensation of occupying a moment without the habitual compulsion to document it, analyze it, or escape into the next thing.

We've built entire architectures of distraction, haven't we? The human capacity for mental time travel—replaying conversations from 2007, rehearsing arguments that will never happen, scrolling through the infinite feed of other people's curated intensities—this capacity has become our prison. The Buddhists call it *dukha*, this background hum of unsatisfactoriness. The Stoics called it *tarache*, disturbance. We call it Tuesday.

But standing in that light, something else emerged. Not enlightenment, certainly. Nothing so grandiose. Just... a loosening. The recognition that I was breathing, that my heart was beating without my supervision, that somewhere nearby a squirrel was making decisions about acorn storage with complete commitment to the task at hand. The squirrel was not worried about squirrel politics, about whether other squirrels were achieving more impressive acorn portfolios, about the ultimate meaning of squirrel existence in a universe of indifferent stars. The squirrel was simply *squirreling*, and in that moment, I was simply humaning, and it was enough.

I think often about the Japanese concept of *wabi-sabi*—the aesthetic of imperfection, impermanence, and incompleteness. A cracked teacup repaired with gold lacquer, the repair becoming the most beautiful part. The way moss claims a stone, neither destroying nor merely decorating but entering into genuine relationship with it. We are so conditioned to seek the unblemished, the permanent, the finished. We want our lives to be like the photographs in magazines that don't exist anymore, flat and glossy and resolved. But reality is textured. Reality has water stains and awkward silences and the particular sadness of refrigerator magnets from trips where you fought the whole time.

There's courage in continuing anyway.

I knew a woman once who kept a garden in soil that everyone told her was useless—too much clay, too little sun, the wrong zone entirely for what she wanted to grow. She amended that soil for years, carting in compost by the wheelbarrow load, planting things that failed, noting the failures, trying again. After a decade, she had created something that looked almost effortless, a lush abundance that visitors admired without understanding the labor of disappointment that underwrote it. "The garden is just the story of what I didn't give up on," she told me once, and I've carried that sentence like a stone in my pocket, smooth from handling.

We don't talk enough about the dignity of maintenance. Our culture fetishizes innovation, disruption, the new new thing. But most of life is upkeep. Brushing teeth. Paying bills. Checking in on friends who are struggling in ways they may not want to discuss. Returning to difficult conversations because the relationship matters more than the comfort of avoidance. These small, repeated acts lack the dramatic arc we crave in our narratives, but they constitute the actual texture of a life well-lived. The marriage that lasts isn't sustained by grand gestures but by ten thousand small generosities, by choosing to load the dishwasher the way your partner prefers even when it seems inefficient, by remembering that their stressful meeting is today and asking about it tonight.

I've been reading about mycelial networks lately—the vast underground fungal connections that link trees in forests, allowing them to share nutrients, send warnings, support the young and the dying. The "wood wide web," some scientists call it, with the slight embarrassment of those who know they're being cute. But there's something profound here about how life actually operates beneath the visible surface. We are so invested in individual achievement, in the hero's journey, in the singular genius. Yet the forest teaches that survival is collaborative, that the mighty oak and the modest fern are in conversation we cannot see, that what looks like competition from aboveground is often cooperation below.

What would it mean to live as if we believed in these connections? Not as metaphor but as material reality? To trust that our small kindnesses travel in channels we don't perceive, that the encouragement we offered a colleague years ago might still be circulating in their system, enabling some present courage? To understand that our cruelties, too, have half-lives longer than we imagine, continuing to decay and release their toxins in ways we never witness?

The late poet Mary Oliver asked, "Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?" It's become a graduation-quote staple, stripped of its urgency through overexposure. But the question remains genuinely devastating if we sit with it honestly. Not what do you plan to achieve, accumulate, become. What do you plan to *do*—this active, present-tense verb—with this singular existence that will not, cannot, come again?

I don't have a satisfying answer. I suspect the question is meant to be lived rather than answered. But I find myself returning to that September light, to the squirrel's acorn deliberations, to the garden built on failures, to the mycelial truth beneath our feet. I think the doing might be simpler than we make it. Pay attention. Stay present. Repair what you can with gold. Keep showing up. Trust the connections you cannot see.

The light changes. It always does. But for a moment, it was perfect, and I was there to notice, and somehow that feels like enough—like more than enough—like the whole point, if there is one, which there probably isn't, which might be the most liberating possibility of all.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
